title: Don't copy the class — extend it
instruction:
You have a `User` class. Now you need `AdminUser` — exactly like `User`, but with extra powers.

Copying the whole class is a maintenance nightmare. Change `User` and you have to remember to change the copy too.

**Inheritance** lets `AdminUser` inherit everything from `User` for free, then only add what's new.

```python
class AdminUser(User):  # AdminUser gets all of User's stuff
```

Run this.
successMessage: AdminUser got greet() and __init__ from User without copying a line. Add a new method to User tomorrow and AdminUser gets it automatically. That's inheritance.

title: super() — extend the parent's setup
instruction:
When `AdminUser` needs its own `__init__` (for extra attributes), use `super()` to run the parent's setup first:

```python
class AdminUser(User):
    def __init__(self, name, email, permissions):
        super().__init__(name, email)  # run User's __init__
        self.permissions = permissions  # then add ours
```

Add a proper `__init__` to `AdminUser` using `super()`.

codeChecks.message: Call super().__init__() to run User's setup first.
Store permissions on self: self.permissions = permissions
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: super().__init__() does User's setup. Then self.permissions adds the admin-specific part. AdminUser gets everything User sets up PLUS its own additions.

title: Override — replace the parent's method
instruction:
A child class can replace a parent's method by defining a method with the same name.

Python always uses the child's version. The parent's is still there if you need it via `super()`.

Override `greet()` in `AdminUser` to include their role.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Python found greet() in AdminUser first — the parent's version is shadowed. Override only what needs to be different; everything else is inherited unchanged.

title: isinstance() — 'is this one of those?'
instruction:
`isinstance(obj, Class)` is True if the object is an instance of that class — or any subclass.

This is how you write flexible code that works with any compatible type:

```python
def process(vehicle):
    if isinstance(vehicle, Vehicle):
        vehicle.start()  # works for Car, Truck, Bus — all vehicles
```

Run this and understand why my_car is a Car, a Vehicle, but not a Truck.
starter:
class Vehicle:
    pass

class Car(Vehicle):
    pass

class Truck(Vehicle):
    pass

my_car = Car()

print(isinstance(my_car, Car))      # True — it is a Car
print(isinstance(my_car, Vehicle))  # True — Car IS a Vehicle
print(isinstance(my_car, Truck))    # False — not a Truck
print(issubclass(Car, Vehicle))     # True — Car is a subclass of Vehicle
successMessage: isinstance(my_car, Vehicle) is True because Car inherits from Vehicle. This is how you write 'accepts any vehicle' functions — check isinstance(Vehicle) and all subclasses work.

title: Multiple inheritance
instruction:
Python lets a class inherit from multiple parents at once:

```python
class FlyingCar(Car, Airplane):
    pass
```

When both parents have the same method, Python picks based on MRO (Method Resolution Order) — left to right.

Run this. Which `move()` wins?
successMessage: Flyable's move() wins — Python goes left to right through the parents. __mro__ shows the lookup order. Multiple inheritance is powerful — use it carefully.

title: Build an animal hierarchy
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a class hierarchy:
- `Animal(name, sound)` — base class with a `speak()` method: `f"{name} says {sound}"`
- `Dog(name)` — inherits from Animal, always sound "Woof"
- `Cat(name)` — inherits from Animal, always sound "Meow"

Create a dog and a cat, call `speak()` on both.

codeChecks.message: Call super().__init__() in both Dog and Cat.

successMessage: Dog and Cat both inherit speak() — no code duplication. Change Animal's speak() and both update automatically. That's the power of inheritance. Chapter 14 done!
